Data.MeaningHint: Picture one Wolverine versus, like, a hundred prisoners. Who would win? Yeah, my money's on the mutant in this conflict.
Data.ReadingHint: Put yourself in Wolverine's shoes. Imagine what it would feel like to have conflicting values, what it would do to your soul. Wanting to be peaceful on the one hand, but on the other not wanting to allow bigotry of any kind. It's a conflict in your soul.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This constant <kanji>conflict</kanji> between Wolverine and the other inmates is really affecting his <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Despite what people think, Wolverine really doesn't like to fight. He just hates injustice so much, he feels he has to do something about it.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: In <radical>prison</radical>, <radical>Wolverine</radical> gets picked on for the <radical>barb</radical>s coming out of his hands. This creates a lot of <kanji>conflict</kanji> between him and the other prisoners. They don't take kindly to mutants, and Wolverine doesn't take kindly to bigots — hence the conflict.

Data.MeaningHint: I'm sure you've heard of flip-flopping in politics, when people try to take both sides of an issue, staying in the middle ground and never taking a stand. Imagine one of these people to represent this kanji.
Data.ReadingHint: Also, this kanji is pronounced exactly like the vocab word 中 (なか). If you can remember that, it'll make remembering 仲 easier.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Imagine a flip-flopping politician you have an average <kanji>relationship</kanji> with. Now imagine that there's a <reading>knocker</reading> (なか) on their forehead and you get to knock that knocker as hard as you like. Maybe you can knock some integrity into them and form a better relationship.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> who takes the <radical>middle</radical> of the road on every issue is likely to have an average <kanji>relationship</kanji> with everyone. He doesn't really take sides in matters, and just stays in the middle. This makes him seem like a wishy-washy people-pleaser, and as such he's only able to maintain average, mid-tier relationships.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture this special cloud floating above your leader. It's probably just a speaker attached to a drone covered in cotton balls.
Data.ReadingHint: You highly doubt this is a magical cloud that can transmit messages. You inch closer to the big dent and try to poke it. Your finger goes right in! Hmm, you're still pretty sure there's a trick to this thing.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As the leader <kanji>transmit</kanji>s his latest series of messages to you and the others, you notice a pretty large <reading>den</reading>t (でん) in it. That dent is probably a clue to what this cloud is really made of.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>cloud</radical> that he uses to <kanji>transmit</kanji> all of the messages he needs to send to his people. The cloud floats above him and when he wants to tell people something he just whispers into the cloud and it transmits his message really, really loudly to all of them.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine a fish trying to hold a full hand of cards together. Impossible! Fish don’t have fingers to keep them spread properly. Now imagine a fish trying to hold two cards together and nodding to hit. It's much easier to keep things together this way!
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine a bunch of fish gathering together in きょうと to participate in this blackjack tournament. With so many fish all together in one place, it probably smells pretty fishy.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Once a year trillions of fish get <kanji>together</kanji> in <reading>きょう</reading>と to play blackjack. They have a huge tournament there once a year.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The card game fish are best at is <radical>blackjack</radical> because they can easily hold the cards with their <radical>fins</radical> <kanji>together</kanji>. In blackjack you don't have to have a huge hand, full of cards. It's just a few, so fish fins can keep them together and just hit or stay.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine a woman and a child you like very much. Just appreciate them, and think about how much you like those two. The reading mnemonic definitely won't ruin your image of them or anything.
Data.ReadingHint: And you DO like them. You have always been a fan of こういち's kanji learning methods, and you feel so lucky to have married a woman who looks just like こういち and then (this is the real miracle!) gave birth to a child that looks like こういち too. Hashtag blessed.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: "I <kanji>like</kanji> you two," you say to the woman and child. They both turn their heads 180 degrees back towards you. They both have faces that look just like <reading>こう</reading>いち's. "We like you too," they say in unison.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: There is a <radical>woman</radical> and a <radical>child</radical> in front of you. You <kanji>like</kanji> them very much.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture the drunkard standing in front of you. He picked up the sword and is holding it above his head. It's becoming... becoming...
Data.ReadingHint:
If a sword becomes a saber in the hands of a drunkard... then what are you? Geez this became pretty deep.

You slice down the drunkard with your saber and become a little more depressed than you were before.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The sword has <kanji>become</kanji> a <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい)! It's just like the saber you have! Hey, wait.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>drunkard</radical> holds a <radical>sword</radical> it <kanji>becomes</kanji> something else. The drunkard doesn't become a swordsman, they're still just a drunkard. But the sword in their hands does.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine yourself digging a coffin hole with nothing but a spoon. Your hands get all cramped, your wrist starts to hurt, and you get painful blisters on your hands. It takes forever to dig a hole big enough for a coffin!
Data.ReadingHint: You hated roe when you were still young, but now that you’re an elderly old fart you really want to eat it. Roe is for old people, you guess, as you slurp it up.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Now that you’re <kanji>elderly</kanji> (and probably about to die) you suddenly crave some delicious <reading>roe</reading> (ろう). Salmon roe, sea urchin roe, tofugu roe, doesn’t matter. Your old, tired bones need roe after all that hard work!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: By the time you finish digging a <radical>coffin</radical> hole with nothing but a <radical>spoon</radical>, you’ll be an old <kanji>elderly</kanji> person. Digging with nothing but a spoon takes a long time, especially when the hole needs to be big enough for a coffin. You might as well be digging it for yourself at this rate. By the time you finish, you’ll be all wrinkled and elderly and just about ready to keel over.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture this great leader. Imagine him actually standing on the shoulders of his supporters who are a lower rank than he is. He’s standing atop this great pyramid as a fearless leader.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture the same ranking pyramid from before, only all the members are boy scouts, and the leader at the top is an eagle. Hear the piercing screech of the eagle with your ears.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This great leader is actually the leader of the boy scouts. And as we all know, the highest <kanji>rank</kanji> in boy scouts is the rank of <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) scout. This great leader has attained the rank of eagle scout and stands above all other scouts.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> <radical>stand</radical>s at the highest <kanji>rank</kanji> in an organization. The leader is of the highest rank and stands above all others.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture yourself, face smooshed into the ground, and you can hear the leader shouting above you, "GET LOW!"
Data.ReadingHint: Get low to the ground right now and ask your mom or dad to roll some tater tots towards your face. Eat them up, mm mm.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Low</kanji> people like you can only eat low food like potatoes. But that's boring, so you low people learned to make <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots. You roll them to each other and eat them without lifting your heads.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of your <radical>clan</radical> wants to live close to the <radical>ground</radical> so he demands you get <kanji>low</kanji>. Low, low, with your belly to the ground. That way you're always below him, since he's the leader.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine the butterflies in your stomach as you prepare to do something for the first time. Now look down at your badass outfit and the sword on your belt. Feel the nervousness subside a little. You've got this.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture yourself strutting down the runway at this fashion show. You're doing pretty well for your first show — until you trip over your sword and fall flat on your face. Darn it — now everyone's gonna know this was your first time.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: So, what is it you're doing for the <kanji>first</kanji> time? A fashion <reading>show</reading> (しょ). Your special outfit and sword aren't just to calm your nerves — they're also what you'll be showing off on the catwalk.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You wear a special <radical>outfit</radical> and equip a <radical>sword</radical> whenever you do something for the <kanji>first</kanji> time. Doing something for the first time is always scary, but the right outfit and a sword at your side can give you the confidence and courage to overcome your jitters!

Data.MeaningHint: Anyone can put a sword and knife in their mouth. But not everyone can make them separate while still in your mouth.
Data.ReadingHint: "I bet you can't separate the sword and knife," they say in an annoying voice. Alright, time for you to show them. Nobody bets you anything without you blindly trying to do it!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Why are you trying to make a sword and knife <kanji>separate</kanji> in your mouth? Because someone <reading>bets</reading> (べつ) you can't do it.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: In your <radical>mouth</radical> you put a <radical>sword</radical> and a <radical>knife</radical>. Now that you have them in there, you have to make them <kanji>separate</kanji> from each other.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine buying some rice. Now cut all the grains with a knife into smaller grains. Now sell them for two, three, four times the profit!
Data.ReadingHint: Picture all of your reeds full of grains in a row. The entire world will benefit from your crafty invention. And you'll turn a great profit too, which certainly doesn't hurt.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: To make an even better <kanji>profit</kanji>, you decide to cut up <reading>ree</reading>ds (り) too. You stuff the grains in the reeds and sell that as "self-cooking" sets.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Cut <radical>grain</radical>s with a <radical>knife</radical> to make them smaller and you'll make a great <kanji>profit</kanji>. By cutting them, now you can get three grains worth of money for what was once a single grain.

Data.MeaningHint:
Imagine you're one of this woman's minions, toiling away in the kitchen. The heat from the stoves is intense, and you're hoping she doesn't see the sweat dripping from your forehead into the food. Man, you've never toiled so hard in your life.

Note: There are a few other kanji with similar meanings. This one is used in words that refer to doing work, with a focus on making a concerted effort.
Data.ReadingHint: Secretly it's this dog you toil for, not the woman. Try to picture what kind of dog it is. And imagine sneaking extra portions to it under the table when the woman isn't looking. Yes, if it weren't for this innocent dog, you'd have done with this life of toil!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As a servant you <kanji>toil</kanji> not just for the woman, but also for her <reading>do</reading>g (ど). Anything she gets, the dog gets too. That means double the toil for you. But hey, at least the dog is cute.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> sits on a <radical>stool</radical> to conserve her <radical>power</radical>, as her servants <kanji>toil</kanji> away in the kitchen. They toil away preparing a feast for this powerful woman while she kicks back on her cozy stool. She's a queen and deserves to be pampered.

Data.MeaningHint:
Imagine yourself as a powerful Viking. You have the power to take anything you want, pillaging and plundering to your heart’s content. Imagine all the things you would acquire with this sort of power.

Note - there are a few other kanji that have a similar meaning to this one. This kanji is used in words related to physical, or manual labor, with an emphasis on how darn tiring it is!
Data.ReadingHint: Picture this powerful Viking laying siege on a small fishing village and taking all the roe and stuffing it into his bearded face. So much labor went into harvesting that roe! Vikings are so mean!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: It takes a lot of <kanji>labor</kanji> to harvest <reading>roe</reading> (ろう) so that’s what the Viking decides to take first! Roe is delicious, but it’s small and delicate. Vikings don’t labor for their own roe! They take it and eat it!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>Viking</radical> in a position of <radical>power</radical> does not need to <kanji>labor</kanji> in the fields. Labor is for the weak! A Viking of power takes what he wants, and need not labor for it. A powerful Viking plunders all and takes what he wants!

Data.MeaningHint: Pretend you are a high ranking officer and imagine yourself giving orders to your underlings. Imagine all the power and influence you have. It feels good.
Data.ReadingHint: Students of American history are very familiar with the Mayflower. Recall learning about it in school whenever you see this kanji. Or look it up on Wikipedia if you don’t know about it. The Mayflower is a big deal!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You are the commanding officer on the <reading>May</reading>flower (めい). You give orders to colonize America. The orders spewing from your mouth hole determine the <kanji>fate</kanji> of the settlers and the fate of America and the fate of the world! You and the Mayflower have changed everything forever.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Orders</radical> coming from your <radical>mouth</radical> will determine the <kanji>fate</kanji> of your soldiers. You are their commanding officer and your orders determine their <kanji>life</kanji> and death.

Data.MeaningHint: You're on top of the mountain and the entire coast is stretching out below. The shore goes as far as your eyes can see.
Data.ReadingHint: Sit down next to Gandhi and close your eyes. You can hear the water hitting the shore below. The coastal birds are screaming. You crack open an eye and see Gandhi still sitting perfectly still.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Sitting up there, watching the <kanji>coast</kanji> with you, is <reading>Gan</reading>dhi (がん). The world famous Gandhi, is just hanging out up here with you, looking down at what you now realize must be a coast in India.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>mountain</radical> on top of the <radical>cliff</radical> is <radical>dry</radical> from the <kanji>coast</kanji> below. Just from looking at the raging <kanji>shore</kanji> below, you'd think everything would be wet, it's splashing everywhere!

Data.MeaningHint: Picture winter heading straight for you and frosting everything over. Now picture the leaves being released by the trees.
Data.ReadingHint: "Release my home from this leaf prison!" you shout at the now bare trees.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The trees <kanji>release</kanji> all of their leaves onto your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). Slowly, you watch in horror as your home is consumed by the released leaves. They enter in through the windows, doors, and the chimney.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: As the year starts moving in the <radical>direction</radical> of <radical>winter</radical>, the trees must <kanji>release</kanji> their leaves.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine yourself playing a never-ending game of blackjack out in the hot sun. You’re sticky, sweaty, and tired. But all you can do is continue to play your game of blackjack. It’s miserable.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine your muck ashi, all dirty and muddy from walking on that old road from long ago. Feel the muck between the toes of your ashi, squishing with every step. Hear the sound the mud makes as it slides between your sweaty mucky toes.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Once you finally finish the game, you walk the long walk back home, in the sun, barefoot, on a dirt road that hasn't been maintained since <kanji>long ago</kanji>. Your feet are all sweaty from being out in the sun, and walking on this dirt road gives you <reading>muck ashi</reading> (むかし). Your ashi (feet) are all mucky and muddy from the combination of dirt and sweat. Your muck ashi are really gross, man.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You've been playing <radical>blackjack</radical> in the <radical>sun</radical> since <kanji>long ago</kanji>. This is the longest game of blackjack you've ever played and it feels like ages since you started. You've been sitting out in the sun playing blackjack since long, long ago.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine being struck by the waves of a tsunami. Feel the force of their impact and the overall sensations on your skin. Those are some mighty waves.
Data.ReadingHint: The waves are basically punching you in the gut, forcing the air out of your lungs, and making you shout "Hah!"
Data.ReadingMnemonic: When each <kanji>wave</kanji> hits your body, you shout, "<reading>Hah</reading> (は)!" It's not like it's on purpose. When the wave crashes into you, the wind gets knocked right out of you, making you say "Hah!"
Data.MeaningMnemonic: What part of a <radical>tsunami</radical> do you feel with your <radical>skin</radical>? The <kanji>wave</kanji>s. Tsunamis are accompanied by all kinds of terrifying sights and sounds, but what you feel on your skin are the powerful waves.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture yourself as this tsunami master. You’re like a water bender from Avatar, only more awesome. Feel the cold wet water pouring out of your fingertips as you create water out of nothing. Imagine what it would be like to have this power.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine Chewbacca soaking wet. All his fur is matted down and he looks pretty depressed. He smells like wet dog too. Smell this smell in your nostrils as you imagine how pathetic this drenched Chewbacca feels.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: If <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう) was trying to attack you, you’d be able to defend yourself easily. Simply <kanji>pour</kanji> a tsunami on him and Chewbacca would surrender in a heartbeat. Chewbacca doesn’t like having water poured on him, especially not all the water from a tsunami.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> <radical>master</radical> has the power to <kanji>pour</kanji> water wherever he pleases. Tsunami masters are masters over the most powerful water force in the universe, so it makes sense that they have the ability to pour water at will, wherever they please. Best not tick off the master, unless you want a tsunami poured on your head.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture the moon raising this ball of trash, giving it love and attention.
Data.ReadingHint: Watch as the little trash ball 行くs up into the sky and floats around its moon mama. If you raise your child right it will want to 行く wherever you go too.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The moon <kanji>nurture</kanji>s the trash ball until one day, it speaks! "<reading>行く</reading> (いく), 行く！" it says. It wants to go live with mom moon!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Give <radical>trash</radical> to the <radical>moon</radical> and it will <kanji>nurture</kanji> and <kanji>raise</kanji> that trash like it's its own little moon baby.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine finding something that suits your life and your style. You're not just going to leave it there, right? No, you're going to pick it up! Go on, use your fingers and pick up that thing.
Data.ReadingHint: You picked up the hero sword and now you're the new hero. Time to go kill a dragon. Good luck, and try not to die, hero person!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You're walking around looking for something to <kanji>pick up</kanji> when you see a sword in a rock. Yes, this will suit you. You pick up the sword and people jump out around you and tell you that you are the new <reading>hero</reading> (ひろ)! Uh oh. Maybe you shouldn't have picked this up.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> always seem to <radical>suit</radical> the random things you <kanji>pick up</kanji> off the ground. An old thimble. A discarded onion ring. A dead worm you tie in a knot. Your incredible fingers suit them beautifully, almost as if they were jewelry, so of course you pick up all of them.

Data.MeaningHint: Look down at your finger and the burned stumps where the rest of your fingers used to be. It hurts. And you're sad. But at least you still have one finger left.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture sitting with a sheep. It's right across from you, staring into your eyes. It reaches out its sheep finger hoof and you reach out with your finger. They touch. It's magical.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Don't worry, you aren't the only one out there with just one <kanji>finger</kanji>, <reading>shee</reading>p (し) only have one finger too. I mean, they're hooves, which are really just a single animal finger. It's just big and kind of flat.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Try to use your <radical>fingers</radical> to <radical>spoon</radical> out some of the <radical>sun</radical> and you'll come back with just one <kanji>finger</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine how ridiculous a sheep would look all decked out in Western-style cowboy gear. Now imagine it riding a tsunami wave as it screams, "Baa~ baa~ baa~!"
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine yourself eating this Western-style apple pie yogurt. It tastes just like apple pie, only creamier and fruitier. Taste the sweet yogurt in your mouth as you imagine yourself scarfing some down.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: After a whole day of riding this tsunami, the sheep is hankerin' for some <kanji>Western-style</kanji> food, namely <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). Yogurt is Western-style food, right? If it helps you can imagine the yogurt is flavored like apple pie, to make it more American and Western.
Data.MeaningMnemonic:
This <radical>tsunami</radical> is ridden by a <radical>sheep</radical> in classic <kanji>Western style</kanji>. And by Western style I mean the sheep is dressed in a cowboy outfit and has put a saddle atop the tsunami wave to ride it. It's a tsunami rodeo and this crazy sheep is riding that tsunami wave in classic Western style.

This kanji also means <kanji>ocean</kanji>, because presumably the people from the West, with their Western style, came from across the ocean. You'll want to be familiar with both meanings for vocab later on.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine a spirit appearing before you. It's a god. You should be as humble as possible. Out of respect and also because you don't want to be cursed or something.
Data.ReadingHint: If you don't get down on your shins before god's spirit, it could take them away. Even your jeans couldn't protect you then. You don't want to live a shin-less life, do you?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: When you meet a <kanji>god</kanji>'s <kanji>spirit</kanji>, you need to get down on your <reading>shin</reading>s (しん). Get down on your ankles? No, not low enough. Knees? No, no, get your shins flat on the ground to show how humble you really are. Luckily, you're wearing <reading>jean</reading>s (じん), so at least that will stop your poor shins getting chafed!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>spirit</radical> should feel <radical>humble</radical> before a <kanji>god</kanji>'s <kanji>spirit</kanji>. You never really feel humble in front of anyone else, but if you meet a god's spirit… Well, you should probably lower yourself appropriately. Gods have powerful spirits.

Data.MeaningHint: Normally an hourglass has sand to count the seconds, but yours uses grains. Imagine just a few grains left, and watch them trickle down as you count a second, and then another second.
Data.ReadingHint: Your hourglass timer was a timer to specifically let you know when your B.O. was set to return.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: After the final <kanji>second</kanji>s go by, your awful <reading>B.O.</reading> (びょう) appears.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Grain</radical>s are <radical>few</radical>. Imagine the last few grains trickling down the hourglass. You only have a <kanji>second</kanji> left.

Data.MeaningHint: When you level up your Japanese skills, you'll take this escalator to rank up too!
Data.ReadingHint: A teacher comes over and says, "Excuse me, this is advanced level cucumber studies. I don't think you're at this level yet... please take the escalator down and follow the threads to find your level."
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You want to see what the highest <kanji>level</kanji> is, so you go up the wrong escalator. At the top you find a giant <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: There are pieces of <radical>thread</radical> on each <radical>escalator</radical> showing you what <kanji>level</kanji> they go to in your school. Each escalator takes you to a different <kanji>grade</kanji>, so make sure you follow the right thread or you'll end up in a class that's either way above or way below your level.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture the bear on its scooter with the offending drop of water on its head following really closely behind your scooter. You can almost feel his bear breath on your neck!
Data.ReadingHint: Picture the bear chasing you on its scooter, trying to reach out and grab the obi off your waist. The drop of water on its head is jiggling from the exertion. Woah, it's getting a little too close!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The bear is trying to <kanji>follow</kanji> you because it wants your <reading>o</reading>bi (お) as compensation for its injured pride. The obi is waving in the wind behind your scooter, and the bear is reaching out with its teeth to try and grab it.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: On a <radical>scooter</radical> there is a <radical>bear</radical> with a <radical>drop</radical> on its head trying to <kanji>follow</kanji> and <kanji>chase</kanji> you! You accidentally splashed it with water and it's not happy about it. You're on your own scooter, luckily, but that doesn't make this any less terrifying.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine what the simple drunkard does, to start the war. They're not in their right mind, and the centaurs (getting you ready for the reading mnemonic!) are an easily offended people. Go ahead and run through the simple drunkard's actions toward the centaurs that start this war.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine the war: humans vs centaurs. Tens of thousands clashing in battle.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The simple drunkard started a <kanji>war</kanji> with the <reading>cen</reading>taurs (せん)! This is especially unfortunate, because the centaurs are really good at <kanji>battle</kanji>s because they're all automatically cavalry troops.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>simple</radical> <radical>drunkard</radical> starting a <kanji>war</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine yourself standing on your older brother. They push you off. So you do it again. You want to be on top, but your older brother doesn't want you to. You compete for the top spot over and over again, throughout your whole life, because that's what one does with their older brother.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine yourself competing to stand on top of your older brother. Do you win? Do you get the one ticket to きょうと?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>compete</kanji> with your older brother because only one of you may go to <reading>きょう</reading>と. Whoever stands on top the most gets to go to きょうと, so you must compete for a spot.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Stand</radical> on your <radical>older brother</radical>. Now <radical>stand</radical> on your <radical>older brother</radical> again. You wish to stand on, and above, your older brother. You <kanji>compete</kanji> over and over again, to stand atop your older brother.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine putting a little drop of water over a root you want to grow. Doing this is good for the plant and also good for your soul.
Data.ReadingHint: Rowing even feels good on your arms and back. Probably just as good as all these roots will feel.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: A <kanji>good</kanji> way to get a lot of drops of water for your roots is to buy a <reading>row</reading> boat (りょう). Then just row out to the middle of the lake and plonk all the roots right into the source!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>drop</radical> of water to a <radical>root</radical> is <kanji>good</kanji>. It's good for the roots to grow and live and thrive. And what's good for roots is good for our plants and us humans too.

Data.MeaningHint: Just constructing this thing was an achievement. Feel proud like it was, well, an achievement.
Data.ReadingHint:
Thanks こういち. Now everyone will know of your achievements around kanji learning and inside-out earth theory.

Hint: When you see 工 in a kanji it will often take the こう reading. Remember that, because it comes up quite a bit!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>achievement</kanji> was made possible by <reading>こう</reading>いち, so of course this construction project is a giant statue of the man himself. What an achievement of a statue. What an achievement of an achievement.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Using your <radical>construction</radical> <radical>power</radical> you build something great. A true <kanji>achievement</kanji> built for the achievement of making kanji learning easier!

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a temple dedicated to a single cow. All of the people who come and worship here have a special place in their hearts for the cow.
Data.ReadingHint: You walk up to a statue of the special cow in the temple and see a collar around its neck that says "Tokugawa Moo Moo" on it. How... special!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This cow is <kanji>special</kanji> because it belonged to <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu, the first shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate and uniter of Japan! He's a super important figure in Japanese history. You should absolutely look him up and read a bit about him (when you're caught up on these lessons, that is).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>cow</radical> <radical>temple</radical> is <kanji>special</kanji>. This cow must have been very important to have an entire temple dedicated to it.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine the leader looking around the rice paddy. She keeps saying, "Ugh, it would be convenient if I didn't have to walk around out here looking for the treasure that's going to make things more convenient for me. Ugh." Go ahead and say this phrase out loud, too, pretending to be the leader in the rice paddy. Feel the cold and the wet, making it less convenient for you.
Data.ReadingHint: Feel the happiness that the leader would feel at this revelation. It will make everything much more convenient for her, since she's not the one doing all the manual labor. She won't have to eat out of a bento, that's for sure.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: She finds a place to dig in the ground. It looks like there's some fresh dirt here. Finally, the treasure of <kanji>convenience</kanji> that will make this leader's life so much easier. She digs into the ground and pulls out a box. When she opens it, she finds out it's a <reading>ben</reading>to (べん). "My god," she thinks. "This really will make my life more convenient. My people can take their lunches with them now instead of coming back to base to eat, which will waste way less time!"
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> stands upon the <radical>ground</radical> of the <radical>rice paddy</radical>. She is searching for <radical>treasure</radical>. It is said that this treasure here in this rice field will make things much easier for the leader (that's what leaders really want, after all). If she gets this treasure, it will be a great <kanji>convenience</kanji> for her, because convenience is everything when you're the leader.

Data.MeaningHint:
Picture this leader asking you (yet again) to work for them. "Can you move those heavy objects with all that power of yours?" Typical leader — as soon as they know you're good at a certain kind of work, they ask you to do it all the time. 

Note: There are a few other kanji that have a similar meaning to this one. This kanji is used in words that refer to doing work, with a focus on movement or operating something.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture yourself receiving a doughnut as payment after you've been working. It's delicious. Doughnuts are fantastic. Taste the fresh doughnut in your mouth as it crumbles and melts on your tongue. It's the best doughnut you've ever had.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Where do you get the power to do all this <kanji>work</kanji>? From <reading>dou</reading>ghnuts (どう). You eat nothing but doughnuts. If you had no doughnuts, you couldn't work. In fact, your leader pays you for your work… in doughnuts.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> makes you lift <radical>heavy</radical> loads with all your <radical>power</radical>, since this is what you do for <kanji>work</kanji>. Why do you do this kind of work? Well, you're good at it and the leader makes you do it, I guess.

Data.MeaningHint: Here's an order: study your radicals.
Data.ReadingHint:
Imagine an authority figure giving you this order as an official order. Ray guns don't really exist, so you think it's a pretty strange order.

Hint: when you see the <radical>orders</radical> radical, the reading will often be れい. The mnemonic will likely involve orders to get a ray gun of some kind.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>orders</kanji> are to get a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you know the radical meaning <radical>orders</radical> you know the kanji meaning <kanji>orders</kanji> as well!

Data.MeaningHint: Put your hand over your heart, or on your wrist, or somewhere you can feel your pulse. Your heart is pumping blood through your body, hear the sound of your heart beat and try to picture and get an idea for how all the blood flows through your body.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture the majestic animal that is the bald eagle. Imagine the sound of an eagle screeching as it zooms through the air, flapping its wings in the wind. It’s pretty impressive.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The heart of an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) is very strong. The person who knew this probably came up with the <kanji>idea</kanji> to make the bald eagle a symbol of the United States. Eagles are always flying around and doing things, so their hearts are very strong.
Data.MeaningMnemonic:
The <radical>sound</radical> of your <radical>heart</radical> gives me an <kanji>idea</kanji>. The methodic thump thump thumping of a heart puts me in a trance that gives me great ideas. It’s a very inspirational sound, the sound of a heart is. It gives me an idea. 

Alternatively, you could look at this kanji and see a "stand" radical and something that kind of (almost) looks like the kanji for "think." When you stand up and think, you come up with your very own <kanji>idea</kanji>s!

Data.MeaningHint: What's your favorite food? Imagine that, as you bite into it, tiny jets are released from inside the food to attack your tongue with flavor bombs, stimulating your sense of taste.
Data.ReadingHint: Of course, if you're vegetarian imagine the flavor of the meat being terrible. Up to you. Make the mnemonic work for your lifestyle.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: And what has more <kanji>flavor</kanji> than a big slab of <reading>me</reading>at (み)? You love meat and its flavor nearly as much as you love the <kanji>taste</kanji> of its juice. So many good things to put in your mouth!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: In your <radical>mouth</radical> there is a <radical>jet</radical> dropping <kanji>flavor</kanji> bombs. Every time you <kanji>taste</kanji> something, it's actually because a tiny jet has flown into your mouth to bomb you with flavor.

Data.MeaningHint: Say "who" over and over again, demanding someone says "turkey," for some reason.
Data.ReadingHint: You're so mad at that person even though you demanded someone say turkey.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Then, someone says turkey. "<kanji>Who</kanji> said turkey? You? How <reading>dare</reading> (だれ) you."
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Who will <radical>say</radical> <radical>turkey</radical>? Who? <kanji>WHO</kanji>???
